items.title: Acute vs chronic classification
items.contentHtml:
Acute and chronic classification

  • Acute mesenteric ischemia: abrupt onset, abdominal pain disproportionate to examination, requires urgent revascularization; mortality >50%.
  • Chronic mesenteric ischemia: progressive arterial insufficiency, postprandial pain, fear of eating, weight loss; typically due to atherosclerosis of at least two of the three visceral trunks.
  • Abdominal angina is the clinical equivalent of chronic ischemia; The abdominal murmur is a non-specific finding.
  • Trap: the absence of peritoneal signs does not rule out acute ischemia in the initial stages.

items.title: Atrial embolism as the main cause
items.contentHtml:
Most common cause: atrial embolism

  • Atrial embolism (atrial fibrillation, mitral valve disease, previous AMI) is the most common cause of acute mesenteric ischemia, representing ~50% of cases.
  • The embolus usually lodges in the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), 3-10 cm from the origin, respecting the ostium.
  • The onset is sudden, with abdominal pain disproportionate to the examination and rapid intestinal emptying (nausea, diarrhea).
  • Trap: in patients without atrial fibrillation, suspect thrombosis in situ over atherosclerotic plaque.

items.foco: Abdominal pain disproportionate to the examination
items.title: disproportionate pain
items.contentHtml:
Abdominal pain disproportionate to the examination

  • It is the cardinal clinical sign of acute mesenteric ischemia: intense pain, of sudden onset, with a soft abdomen and without peritoneal signs in the first hours.
  • The discrepancy between the pain and the examination is due to the fact that the intestine has not yet suffered transmural necrosis.
  • When peritoneal signs appear (stiffness, rebound), viability The intestinal tract is already compromised and mortality is increased.
  • In non-occlusive mesenteric ischemia the pain may be more insidious, but the disproportion persists as a diagnostic guide.

items.title: Fingerprint sign
items.contentHtml:
Fingerprint sign on computed tomography

  • The fingerprint sign (or thumbprinting) is a finding on contrast-enhanced computed tomography that corresponds to edema of the intestinal wall, with thickening and prominent folds.
  • It is characteristic of acute mesenteric ischemia in early stages, although it is also seen in inflammatory or infectious colitis (Key differential diagnosis).
  • In ischemia, the thickening is usually hypodense (edema) and can be associated with intestinal pneumatosis (gas in the wall) in advanced stages.
  • Trap: the absence of fingerprint sign does not rule out ischemia; decreased mural attenuation or perfusion defect are more specific.

items.foco: Portal venous gas as a late finding
items.title: portal venous gas
items.contentHtml:
Portal venous gas as a late finding

  • Portal venous gas is a late finding on computed tomography that indicates transmural intestinal necrosis and a poor prognosis.
  • It is caused by the passage of gas from the intestinal lumen to the portal circulation through the necrotic wall.
  • It is a sign of severity that requires urgent laparotomy; its presence is associated with mortality >70%.
  • Differential diagnosis: portal venous gas can also be seen in severe ischemic colitis, intra-abdominal sepsis or after endoscopic procedures, but in the context of acute pain it is almost pathognomonic of advanced ischemia.

items.title: Lactate and metabolic acidosis
items.contentHtml:
Elevated lactate and metabolic acidosis

  • Serum lactate >2 mmol/L and metabolic acidosis (pH <7.35, HCO3 <22) are markers of advanced intestinal ischemia, but have low sensitivity in the initial phases.
  • Elevated lactate is due to tissue hypoperfusion and anaerobic metabolism; a normal lactate does not rule out acute ischemia.
  • Metabolic acidosis with elevated GAP anion indicates extensive necrosis and is an adverse prognostic factor.
  • Trap: other processes (shock, sepsis, ketoacidosis) elevate lactate; clinical-radiological correlation is mandatory.

items.title: Mesenteric angiography
items.contentHtml:
Mesenteric angiography as the gold standard

  • Mesenteric angiography continues to be the diagnostic gold standard, although computed tomography angiography has displaced it as the first line due to its speed and less invasiveness.
  • It allows visualization of the embolus or thrombus, assessment of collateral flow and, in the same procedure, performance of endovascular treatment (thrombectomy, angioplasty, infusion of vasodilators).
  • Main indication: suspicion of non-occlusive mesenteric ischemia or when computed tomography angiography is inconclusive.
  • Trap: in in situ thrombosis angiography shows proximal occlusion in the ostium of the SMA, unlike embolism that respects the origin.

items.title: Thromboembolism vs in situ thrombosis
items.contentHtml:
Thromboembolism vs in situ thrombosis

  • Thromboembolism: cardiac origin (atrial fibrillation, valvular disease), sudden occlusion 3-10 cm from the origin of the SMA, respecting the ostium; preserved collaterals.
  • Thrombosis in situ: on atherosclerotic plaque, proximal occlusion in the ostium of the SMA, with a history of chronic mesenteric ischemia (abdominal angina).
  • thrombosis in situ is usually associated with diffuse atherosclerotic disease (coronary, carotid, peripheral).
  • differential diagnosis angiographic: in embolism the contrast stops abruptly in a distal vessel; In thrombosis there is occlusion at the origin with little collateral flow.

items.foco: Non-occlusive mesenteric ischemia due to low output
items.title: Non-occlusive mesenteric ischemia
items.contentHtml:
Non-occlusive mesenteric ischemia due to low output

  • Non-occlusive mesenteric ischemia (NOMI) is caused by low cardiac output (shock, sepsis, AMI, hemorrhage) without visible arterial occlusion.
  • Pathophysiology: sustained splanchnic vasoconstriction, which reduces mesenteric flow; It predominates in the territories of the superior mesenteric artery and inferior mesenteric artery.
  • Clinics: insidious abdominal pain, distention, ileus; Diagnosis requires mesenteric angiography showing thin, irregular vessels and spasm.
  • Treatment: correct the cause (volume, inotropes) and intra-arterial infusion of papaverine (first-line vasodilator).

items.title: Superior mesenteric artery syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Superior mesenteric artery syndrome

  • Superior mesenteric artery syndrome (SAMS) is an extrinsic compression of the third portion of the duodenum due to the narrow aorto-mesenteric angle (<25°).
  • It is not ischemia, but rather a mechanical duodenal obstruction; manifests with postprandial vomiting, epigastric pain and weight loss.
  • Predisposing factors: rapid weight loss, scoliosis, spinal surgery, reduced aorto-mesenteric angle on computed tomography.
  • Initial treatment: nutritional support and postural measures; if unsuccessful, laparoscopic or open duodeno-jejunostomy.

items.title: Urgent surgical treatment
items.contentHtml:
Urgent surgical treatment with resection

  • In the event of peritoneal signs or confirmed necrosis, urgent laparotomy is mandatory: intestinal resection of non-viable segments and revascularization (embolectomy, bypass) are performed.
  • Intestinal viability is evaluated with intravenous fluorescein and Wood's light, or with Doppler. intraoperative
  • Second-look strategy: re-exploration after 24-48 hours to assess new necrosis and avoid initial massive resections.
  • The high mortality greater than 50% is reduced if revascularization is performed before transmural necrosis (first 6-12 hours from the beginning).

items.title: High mortality
items.contentHtml:
High mortality greater than 50%

  • The high mortality greater than 50% in acute mesenteric ischemia is due to delayed diagnosis and extensive intestinal necrosis.
  • Associated factors: age >70 years, severe metabolic acidosis, portal venous gas, need for massive resection, shock on admission.
  • The high mortality greater than 50% can be reduced to 20-30% if early computed tomography angiography and revascularization are performed within the first 6 hours.
  • Trap: non-occlusive mesenteric ischemia has similar mortality (>50%) because it usually occurs in critically ill patients with severe comorbidities.
